# Polaris Documentation

> User and developer documentation for Polaris, the pETH-powered yield layer for DeFi. Issue pAssets backed entirely by onchain collateral and yield. No T-bills. No CEXs. No compromises.

## Agent Guidance

- Start with [Launch Status](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/launch-status) for what is live, supported, or production-final.
- Treat [Public Testnet 1](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/resources/testnet) as the source of truth for Sepolia network, parameter, and contract-address values.
- Public Testnet 1 assets are Sepolia test assets with no monetary value, no production redemption, and no mainnet claim.
- Do not infer production addresses, production constants, audit status, or launch status from testnet values.
- Prefer the Markdown URLs in this file for retrieval, and cite the canonical URL shown beside each page.

## Polaris

URL: https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/
Markdown URL: https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/index.md

Polaris is a DeFi yield layer built around **pETH**, a yield-bearing collateral asset purposefully designed to back Polaris stablecoins and other synthetic assets.

ETH enters the system through the Polaris bonding curve, pETH becomes the core reserve asset, and protocol activity routes value back into the system. This creates a foundation for onchain money and synthetic assets that does not depend on T-bills, centralized exchanges, custodians, or offchain black-box agreements.

At the center of Polaris are three simple pieces:

- **pETH**, the ETH-backed reserve and collateral asset.
- **pAssets**, decentralized currencies and synthetic assets backed by pETH.
- **POLAR**, the stewardship token that aligns long-term incentives.

Polaris is currently in Public Testnet 1 on Sepolia. Start with the quickstart if you want to try the app, or continue below for the high-level system view.

## Launch Timeline

Image: Polaris launch timeline. Early Research, 2024. Team Formation, June 2025. Private Testnet 1, March 2026. Public Testnet 1, May 2026, the current phase. Audits, Planned. Mainnet Launch, Forthcoming. pAsset Expansion, Planned. Integrations, Planned. Future Products, Exploring. Polaris is currently in Public Testnet 1 on Sepolia. The roadmap continues through audits, mainnet launch, pAsset expansion, integrations, and future products.

Image: Polaris system overview: ETH swaps into pETH on the bonding curve, pETH collateralizes pAsset markets that issue pUSD, and burning pETH for POLAR raises the floor and releases ETH.

## The Three Engines

Three tokens work in harmony to sustain Polaris.

Engine | What it is | Why it matters

**pETH** | A yield-bearing reserve asset backed by ETH and powered by the Polaris bonding curve | Provides capital-efficient, yield-bearing collateral with a natively growing floor value
**pAssets** | Decentralized currencies and synthetic assets backed by pETH | Enable scalable onchain money without custodians or external counterparties
**POLAR** | The stewardship token that governs key protocol parameters and captures protocol value | Aligns long-term incentives while keeping the protocol decentralized

Next steps:
- [Public Testnet Quickstart](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/quickstart): The shortest safe path from a wallet to a first Polaris testnet action.
- [App Guide](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/using-app): A compact map of the Dashboard, Swap, Borrow, Earn, Split, Zap, and Advanced tabs.
- [Why Polaris?](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/why-polaris): The design choices behind Polaris and why they matter.

Relevant app/search vocabulary: app, official app, testnet, Public Testnet 1, Sepolia, pETH, pAssets.

## Polaris Launch Status

URL: https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/launch-status

## Current Phase

Item | Status

Public phase | **Public Testnet 1**
Testnet network | **Sepolia**, chain ID `11155111`
Official testnet app | [https://app.testnet.polarisfinance.io/](https://app.testnet.polarisfinance.io/)
Testnet-active pAssets | pUSD and pGOLD
Production network | Ethereum mainnet, forthcoming

## What This Means

- Polaris is currently available on Public Testnet 1 using Sepolia test assets only.
- Testnet assets have no monetary value, redemption rights, or claim on future mainnet assets.
- Do not rely on production infrastructure, audits, bounty programs, or mainnet workflows unless explicitly marked as live.
- Sepolia deployments may change as testing progresses.

## Start Here

If you want to use the app now, follow [Public Testnet Quickstart](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/quickstart). If anything in your wallet prompt conflicts with this page, stop and verify before signing.

Relevant app/search vocabulary: app, official app, testnet app, app.testnet.polarisfinance.io, connect wallet, testnet, Public Testnet 1, Sepolia, Sepolia ETH, chain ID 11155111, WETH faucet, borrow, earn, swap, contracts, audits.

## Public Testnet Quickstart

URL: https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/quickstart
Markdown URL: https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/quickstart.md

Use this page to try Polaris on Public Testnet 1.

1. Open [https://app.testnet.polarisfinance.io/](https://app.testnet.polarisfinance.io/).
2. Connect your wallet on Sepolia, chain ID `11155111`.
3. Get Sepolia ETH for gas from a Sepolia faucet if needed.
4. Claim test WETH from the Dashboard faucet.
5. Use [Swap](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/using-app/swap) to buy pETH on the bonding curve.
6. Use [Borrow](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/using-app/borrow) to issue pUSD or pGOLD against pETH.
7. Use [Earn](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/using-app/earn) to deposit pAssets into an Earn Vault.
8. Return to the [Dashboard](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/using-app) to confirm your positions and rewards.

## Success Checks

Check | Expected result

Network | Wallet shows Sepolia, chain ID `11155111`
Frontend | URL is exactly `https://app.testnet.polarisfinance.io/`
Assets | Balances are testnet assets only
pETH | pETH balance updates after swap
Borrow | pUSD or pGOLD position appears after issuing
Earn | Earn Vault deposit appears after deposit or zap

For failed transactions, missing balances, faucet limits, or wrong-network issues, see [Troubleshooting](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/troubleshooting).

Relevant app/search vocabulary: quickstart, troubleshooting, wrong network, Sepolia, faucet, approval, stale quote, failed transaction.

---

## Dashboard

URL: https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/using-app
Markdown URL: https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/using-app.md

The Dashboard is your starting point in Polaris. It shows your balances, active positions, Earn Vault opportunities, staking rewards, recent activity, and the testnet faucet.

Use it to:

- Connect your wallet.
- Claim test WETH on Sepolia.
- Check your pETH, pAsset, POLAR, fpETH, and vpETH balances.
- Review Earn Vault and staking opportunities.
- Confirm recent transactions and position changes.

Image: Polaris Dashboard ("Your Polaris") showing Earn Vault and staking APR cards, the connect-faucet-earn onboarding steps, and the token price ticker

The Dashboard is an overview screen. To take action, use [Swap](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/using-app/swap), [Borrow](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/using-app/borrow), [Earn](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/using-app/earn), [Split](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/using-app/split), [Zap](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/using-app/zap), or [Advanced](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/using-app/advanced).

---

## Advanced Tools in the Polaris App

URL: https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/using-app/advanced
Markdown URL: https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/using-app/advanced.md

The Advanced section gives direct access to a few core Polaris mechanisms for arbitrageurs and keepers. These tools are fully permissionless and offer more flexibility, but they assume some familiarity with how the protocol works.

It currently includes:

- **Peg Stability Module (PSM):** issue or redeem pUSD and pGOLD against pETH at the oracle price, with no size limits. Issuing exchanges pETH for the selected asset, while redemption swaps the asset back into pETH. This arbitrage helps maintain each peg.
- **Convert pETH → POLAR:** exchange pETH for newly created POLAR through a one-way auction. Converted pETH is permanently burned, increasing the bonding curve floor, while the auction price gradually decays until conversion occurs. In this case, there is no reverse direction.
- **Testnet ETH Faucet:** claim test WETH using Sepolia ETH, subject to the current rate, cooldown period, and faucet limits.

Image: Polaris Advanced tab ("Expert operations") with the PSM Mint and Redeem panels for pUSD and pGOLD

## Basic Flow

1. Pick the module: PSM mint/redeem, pETH → POLAR conversion, or faucet.
2. For PSM, choose pUSD or pGOLD and the direction; review the output and fee at the oracle price.
3. For conversion, review the current auction price against the market POLAR price before converting.
4. Approve the input token if required, then sign.
5. Confirm the resulting balance and that no unexpected allowance remains.

Use the guided [Borrow](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/using-app/borrow), [Earn](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/using-app/earn), [Split](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/using-app/split), or [Swap](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/using-app/swap) page when possible. Use [Troubleshooting](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/troubleshooting) before retrying a failed transaction.

---

## Borrow in the Polaris App

URL: https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/using-app/borrow
Markdown URL: https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/using-app/borrow.md

Borrow is where you issue pAssets against pETH or borrow ETH against fpETH.

- **pUSD and pGOLD:** deposit pETH collateral and issue overcollateralized assets against your position.
- **ETH:** borrow ETH against fpETH at up to 100% LTV with no liquidation risk, as the fpETH floor only ever rises.

Once connected, you can manage existing positions from this tab, including adjustments, repayments, and closures.

Image: Polaris Borrow tab with pUSD, pGOLD, and ETH sub-tabs, a collateral field, LTV slider, effective rate, and a projected-position panel

## Basic Flow

1. Choose the sub-tab: pUSD, pGOLD, or ETH.
2. Enter collateral and the amount you want to borrow.
3. Review LTV, liquidation price, expected cost, and incentives.
4. Approve the collateral token if needed.
5. Sign the transaction.
6. Confirm the updated position in the Dashboard or Borrow tab.

## How Your Position Changes Over Time

Your position is affected by more than the amount you borrow.

- Your pETH collateral has exposure to ETH through the bonding curve.
- Your position can receive pETH incentives based on your issued debt.
- Your position can be redeemed against when pAssets trade below their peg.
- Your liquidation price changes as collateral value, debt, and interest move.

Borrowing is not just a one-time transaction. Monitor your position and keep a clear buffer below the maximum LTV.

For disabled actions or reverted transactions, see [Troubleshooting](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/troubleshooting). For position risk, see [Risk Disclosure](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/resources/risk-disclosure).

Relevant app/search vocabulary: Borrow, Manage Borrow Position, borrow, trove, troves, open trove, manage trove, collateralized debt position, CDP, issue, open borrow position, repay, close borrow position, pUSD, pGOLD, LTV, loan-to-value, official app.

---

## Earn in the Polaris App

URL: https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/using-app/earn
Markdown URL: https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/using-app/earn.md

Earn is where you deposit assets into Polaris yield opportunities.

Current opportunities include:

- pUSD Earn Vaults, where deposits earn from borrower interest and liquidation gains.
- pGOLD Earn Vaults, where deposits earn from borrower interest and liquidation gains.
- POLAR staking, where stakers receive protocol value based on their voting power.
- vpETH rewards, where holders gain exposure to the volatile component of pETH beyond its floor value and participate in pETH rewards distribution.

Earn Vault deposits act as the first line of defense during liquidations. In exchange, depositors receive yield from protocol activity.

Image: Polaris Earn tab with pUSD, pGOLD, POLAR, and vpETH sub-tabs, an APR, a deposit/withdraw toggle, and a claim-pending option

## Basic Flow

1. Select the sub-tab and review its APR, your current deposit, and pending gains.
2. Choose deposit/withdraw (or stake/unstake).
3. Choose whether to claim pending rewards in the same transaction.
4. Approve the token if required, then sign.
5. Confirm your deposit, balance, and claimable gains updated.

For disabled deposits, claims, or staking actions, see [Troubleshooting](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/troubleshooting). For first-loss risk, see [Risk Disclosure](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/resources/risk-disclosure).

Relevant app/search vocabulary: Earn, earn, yield, APR, Earn Vault, deposit, claim rewards.

---

## Split in the Polaris App

URL: https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/using-app/split
Markdown URL: https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/using-app/split.md

**pETH** combines two distinct sources of value: a steadily growing floor (**fpETH**) and a volatile component above that floor (**vpETH**).

Split converts pETH into matching fpETH and vpETH at a fixed **1:1:1** ratio. This creates two assets with distinct risk and return profiles while preserving a path back to pETH.

The process is fully reversible and does not rely on any external dependencies: merge 1 fpETH + 1 vpETH back into 1 pETH at any time. Use this page for the workflow; use [pETH Split](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/peth/split) for the mechanism.

Image: Polaris Split tab with a Split/Merge toggle, fpETH and vpETH outputs, live floor and market prices, and a price simulator

## Basic Flow

1. Choose Split or Merge.
2. Enter the amount and review the expected fpETH + vpETH (or pETH) output.
3. Approve pETH, fpETH, or vpETH if required.
4. Sign the transaction.
5. Confirm wallet balances update.

A split worked when pETH decreases and fpETH plus vpETH each increase by the same amount. A merge worked when equal fpETH and vpETH decrease and pETH increases.

For failed split or merge transactions, see [Troubleshooting](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/troubleshooting).

Relevant app/search vocabulary: Swap, swap, Split, fpETH, vpETH, pETH, floor price.

---

## Swap in the Polaris App

URL: https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/using-app/swap
Markdown URL: https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/using-app/swap.md

Swap lets you exchange test WETH for pETH through the Polaris bonding curve.

pETH is the core collateral asset in Polaris. Once you hold pETH, you can use it to issue pAssets, split it into fpETH and vpETH, or participate in other protocol strategies.

Image: Polaris Swap tab showing the Sell/Buy direction, the WETH to pETH bonding-curve route, the quote, and slippage controls

Before signing, check the quoted price, expected output, and slippage settings.

## Basic Flow

1. Choose the direction: buy pETH with WETH, or sell pETH for WETH.
2. Enter the amount and review the quote and route.
3. Check minimum received, fees, and slippage.
4. Approve the input token if prompted.
5. Sign the swap transaction.
6. Confirm your pETH or WETH balance updates.

For stale quotes or reverted swaps, see [Troubleshooting](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/troubleshooting).

Relevant app/search vocabulary: Swap, swap, Split, fpETH, vpETH, pETH, floor price.

---

## Zap in the Polaris App

URL: https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/using-app/zap
Markdown URL: https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/using-app/zap.md

Zap combines multiple on-chain actions into a single transaction, making it easier to enter common Polaris strategies.

The current strategies are:

- **pUSD Stable Yield:** uses your WETH to acquire pUSD and deposit it into the pUSD Earn Vault in one step, earning yield from issuer interest and liquidation gains.
- **Floor Carry:** converts ETH into fpETH and issues ETH against it, creating a non-liquidatable position that can be looped to increase exposure.

Additional strategies will become available over time.

A zap only simplifies execution. The underlying economics remain exactly the same, and positions can always be managed manually afterward.

Image: Polaris Zap tab showing the pUSD Stable Yield route from WETH through pUSD into the Earn Vault

You keep full self-custody throughout the process. Before signing, a simulation shows the expected result and slippage is set by your settings.

## Basic Flow

1. Choose the strategy.
2. Enter the input amount.
3. Review every route leg, the output estimate, slippage, and the deposit or loan target.
4. Let the simulation confirm the expected output.
5. Sign approvals only for expected spenders, then sign the zap.
6. Confirm the final position on the Dashboard, Earn, or Borrow tab.

For stale quotes, failed simulations, or partial setup after approval, see [Troubleshooting](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/troubleshooting).

Relevant app/search vocabulary: Earn, earn, yield, APR, Earn Vault, deposit, claim rewards.

---

## Troubleshooting

URL: https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/troubleshooting
Markdown URL: https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/troubleshooting.md

Use this page when a wallet action, quote, balance, faucet claim, or transaction does not behave as expected.

## Wrong Network

Confirm your wallet is connected to Sepolia, chain ID `11155111`.

## Wrong Frontend

Use only the official Public Testnet 1 app:

[https://app.testnet.polarisfinance.io/](https://app.testnet.polarisfinance.io/)

## Faucet Claim Fails

The faucet may fail if:

- You do not have enough Sepolia ETH.
- Your address is still in cooldown.
- The daily faucet cap has been reached.
- The faucet is paused.

## Balance Missing

Check that:

- You are connected with the same wallet.
- The transaction confirmed.
- You are viewing the correct asset and network.
- The app has refreshed after confirmation.

## Approval Succeeded But Action Failed

Approvals and actions are separate transactions. If approval succeeded, return to the action tab and submit the transaction again after checking the new quote.

## Quote Changed

Quotes can change when protocol state moves. Refresh the quote before signing.

## Borrow Action Fails

Common causes:

- LTV is too high.
- Minimum debt or collateral requirements are not met.
- The position would become unsafe after the action.
- Recovery rules restrict the action.

## Earn Action Fails

Common causes:

- Insufficient pAsset balance.
- Approval has not been completed.
- The selected Earn Vault is unavailable.

## Split Or Merge Fails

Common causes:

- Insufficient pETH, fpETH, or vpETH balance.
- Approval has not been completed.
- The transaction quote expired.

## Transaction Pending Or Reverted

Check gas settings, wallet status, and the transaction on Sepolia Etherscan. If the transaction reverted, refresh the app and get a new quote before retrying.

Relevant app/search vocabulary: quickstart, troubleshooting, wrong network, Sepolia, faucet, approval, stale quote, failed transaction.

---

## Issuing pAssets

URL: https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/minting
Markdown URL: https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/minting.md

Polaris lets users issue pAssets directly from the protocol.

To issue pUSD or pGOLD, users deposit pETH as collateral and create an overcollateralized position. The issued asset can then be used across Polaris and the wider DeFi ecosystem.

This is similar in spirit to borrowing from a money market: you provide collateral, receive an asset, and manage your position over time. The difference is that Polaris uses pETH as the reserve asset and routes protocol activity back into the system.

## What To Consider Before Issuing

Before issuing pAssets, check:

- Your LTV and liquidation price.
- How pETH price exposure affects your collateral.
- How pETH incentives may accrue based on your issued debt.
- How redemptions can affect your position when pAssets trade below peg.
- Whether the system is in a normal or recovery state.

Issuing pAssets creates an active position. You should monitor it over time.

## Basic Flow

1. Acquire pETH through [Swap](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/using-app/swap) or another supported route.
2. Open [Borrow](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/using-app/borrow).
3. Choose pUSD or pGOLD.
4. Enter pETH collateral and the amount to issue.
5. Review LTV, liquidation price, expected cost, and incentives.
6. Approve pETH if needed, then sign.

For current LTV thresholds and fees, see [Parameters](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/resources/testnet#parameters). For the full risk model, see [Risk Disclosure](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/resources/risk-disclosure).

Next steps:
- [Open a Position](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/minting/open-a-trove): Issue your first pAsset against pETH collateral from the app.
- [Manage a Position](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/minting/managing-your-trove): Monitor LTV, interest, redemptions, and repayment.
- [pAsset Catalog](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/minting/passet-catalog): See which pAssets are testnet-active and which are future examples.

---

## Manage a Position

URL: https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/minting/managing-your-trove
Markdown URL: https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/minting/managing-your-trove.md

After you [open a position](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/minting/open-a-trove), you can adjust it: add collateral, issue more, repay debt, withdraw collateral, or close. The main app-facing health metric is **loan-to-value (LTV)**.

## LTV

LTV is the recorded value of your debt divided by the value of your collateral:

> LTV = debt face value ÷ collateral value

Lower LTV is safer. Some protocol references use collateral ratio instead, which is the reciprocal:

> ICR = collateral value ÷ debt face value

That means 150% collateral ratio is the same position as 66.7% LTV. Two rules matter:

- Above the maximum LTV, the position can be [liquidated](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/redemptions-liquidations/liquidations).
- [Redemptions](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/redemptions-liquidations) can reduce collateral and debt across open positions when a pAsset trades below peg.

Your LTV can move even if you do nothing, because prices, rates, redemptions, and rewards keep changing.

## Position Health Simulator

The simulator below defaults to the current Public Testnet 1 MCR of 115%, equivalent to about 87.0% max LTV. Always verify live values on [Parameters](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/resources/testnet#parameters) and in the app before signing.

## Basic Actions

- **Add collateral:** lowers LTV without changing debt.
- **Issue more:** raises LTV and reduces your safety buffer.
- **Repay debt:** lowers LTV and frees room to withdraw collateral.
- **Close:** repay the full debt, then withdraw collateral.
- **Transfer:** positions are represented by an NFT. Transferring it transfers control of collateral, debt, and management rights.

## What To Watch

- Borrow interest increases debt over time.
- Collateral pETH can receive protocol incentives while it backs debt.
- Redemptions can reduce collateral and debt even while the position is solvent.
- Withdrawing collateral or issuing more debt raises LTV.
- Recovery Mode can restrict risk-increasing actions.

For current thresholds, see [Parameters](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/resources/testnet#parameters). For the full risk model, see [Risk Disclosure](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/resources/risk-disclosure).

Next steps:
- [Open a Position](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/minting/open-a-trove): Issue your first pAsset against pETH collateral.
- [Liquidations](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/redemptions-liquidations/liquidations): Understand what happens if your LTV crosses the maximum.
- [Earn Vaults](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/yield): Earn yield by depositing pAssets.

Relevant app/search vocabulary: Borrow, Manage Borrow Position, borrow, trove, troves, open trove, manage trove, collateralized debt position, CDP, issue, open borrow position, repay, close borrow position, pUSD, pGOLD, LTV, loan-to-value, official app, borrow position, manage borrow position, pAsset debt position.

---

## Open a Position

URL: https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/minting/open-a-trove
Markdown URL: https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/minting/open-a-trove.md

Use Borrow to issue pUSD or pGOLD against pETH.

Image: Polaris Borrow tab with pUSD, pGOLD, and ETH sub-tabs, a collateral field, LTV slider, effective rate, and a projected-position panel

## Basic Flow

1. Open the [Borrow](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/using-app/borrow) tab.
2. Choose pUSD or pGOLD.
3. Enter how much pETH you want to deposit.
4. Choose how much you want to issue.
5. Review LTV, liquidation price, expected cost, and incentives.
6. Approve pETH if required.
7. Sign the transaction.
8. Confirm the position appears in the Borrow tab.

## What To Watch

- Lower LTV gives you more room before liquidation.
- Higher LTV gives you more capital efficiency but less safety.
- Your position can receive pETH incentives based on issued debt.
- Your position can be redeemed against when the issued asset trades below peg.
- You can add collateral, repay debt, or close the position later.

For failed transactions or disabled actions, see [Troubleshooting](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/troubleshooting).

Next steps:
- [Borrow App Guide](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/using-app/borrow): Understand the Borrow tab and how to manage an existing position.
- [Manage a Position](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/minting/managing-your-trove): Adjust, monitor, and close a position.
- [Earn Vaults](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/yield): Earn yield by depositing pAssets.

Relevant app/search vocabulary: Borrow, Manage Borrow Position, borrow, trove, troves, open trove, manage trove, collateralized debt position, CDP, issue, open borrow position, repay, close borrow position, pUSD, pGOLD, LTV, loan-to-value, official app, borrow position, manage borrow position, pAsset debt position.

---

## Polaris Asset Catalog

URL: https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/minting/passet-catalog
Markdown URL: https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/minting/passet-catalog.md

A **pAsset** is a synthetic debt asset issued directly from the Polaris protocol against pETH collateral. **pETH is different:** it is the bonding-curve collateral token that backs pAssets, not a debt asset issued from a borrow position.

> **Current Public Testnet 1 focus:** pUSD and pGOLD are Testnet-active on Sepolia. pCHF, pBigMac, and other catalog ideas are illustrative or future until [Launch Status](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/launch-status) says otherwise.

## Polaris assets

Asset | Tracks | Current status | Notes

**pUSD** | US dollar | **Testnet-active** | The flagship pAsset. See [Issuing pAssets](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/minting).
**pETH** | ETH | **Testnet-active collateral** | *Special.* Not issued from a borrow position: it is the collateral itself, produced by the [bonding curve](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/peth). It backs every other pAsset.
**pGOLD** | 1 ounce of gold | **Testnet-active** | Decentralized gold exposure, backed by pETH rather than bullion. Public Testnet 1 uses Sepolia testnet price-feed plumbing; see the comparison below.
**pCHF** | Swiss franc | **Illustrative / future** | A non-USD fiat peg, demonstrating that the model is debt-asset agnostic across currencies. Not Testnet-active unless Launch Status later lists it.
**pBigMac** | The [Big Mac Index](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Big_Mac_Index) | **Illustrative / future** | An example of a non-financial reference asset. Not Testnet-active unless Launch Status later lists it.

## pGOLD vs centralized gold (XAUT/PAXG)

pGOLD and the incumbent gold tokens both give gold-price exposure, but they are structurally different products.

Property | pGOLD | XAUT / PAXG

Collateral | pETH, fully onchain | Physical gold in custody
Redemption | Permissionless, no protocol-level minimum published yet | Issuer-gated; account requirements and redemption minimums depend on the issuer's current terms
KYC | None | Required to redeem
Physical custody | None - nothing to store or insure | Vaulted bullion, with custody risk
Counterparty | Polaris protocol only | Tether / Paxos as issuer

Image: pGOLD versus centralized gold tokens (XAUT/PAXG): pGOLD is backed by onchain pETH with permissionless, KYC-free access and Polaris as the only counterparty, while XAUT/PAXG are backed by vaulted bullion with issuer-gated, KYC-required redemption and custody risk

pGOLD inherits ETH-collateral and smart-contract risk where XAUT/PAXG inherit custodial and issuer risk. What pGOLD removes is the permissioned issuer layer.

pGOLD's collateral pricing composes two oracle feeds with the bonding curve: pETH is valued in gold terms as ETH/USD × the curve's pETH price ÷ gold/USD. A failure of either feed affects pGOLD pricing; see [Oracle risk](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/resources/risk-disclosure#oracle-risk).

Image: Borrowing pGOLD is not shorting gold: deposit pETH and borrow pGOLD, then your strategy sets the exposure - hold or LP the gold pair for gold-neutral exposure, or sell pGOLD for pETH (or loop) to take short-gold exposure

*Issuing pGOLD against pETH is a borrowing action; whether you end up long pETH, gold-neutral, or short gold depends on what you do with the pGOLD.*

## StablecoinOS: an open catalog

Polaris is **debt-asset agnostic**: it can issue a synthetic pegged to any asset with robust reference pricing and risk parameters. StablecoinOS lets new pAssets share the same pETH collateral pool and bonding curve.

Shared collateral lets teams add a new pAsset without bootstrapping a fresh collateral base. Each pAsset still has its own debt accounting, so stress in one pAsset does not directly liquidate another pAsset's borrow positions.

Image: StablecoinOS as a factory: ETH enters one shared bonding curve to create pETH, which collateralizes multiple independent pAsset markets - pUSD, pGOLD, pCHF - over the same pETH collateral base

Next steps:
- [Open a Position](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/minting/open-a-trove): Issue your first pAsset against pETH collateral, step by step.
- [pETH & the Bonding Curve](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/peth): The collateral primitive every pAsset market shares.
- [Launch Status](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/launch-status): Which pAssets are testnet-active today.

---

## pETH Bonding Curve

URL: https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/peth
Markdown URL: https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/peth.md

The bonding curve is the foundation of Polaris. From this one primitive come pETH, its rising floor, [Split](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/peth/split), reserve loans, and value capture on every interaction.

## What is a bonding curve?

A two-way bonding curve lets you swap like a regular AMM pool, with one key difference: **it needs no liquidity providers.** Instead, it enforces a mathematical relationship between a token's price and its supply:

- Demand rises, tokens are created, price increases.
- Tokens are burned or sold, price decreases.

Buying adds ETH to the reserve and creates pETH. Selling withdraws from the reserve and burns pETH. The result is contract-native liquidity with no external liquidity provider, subject to the execution caveats in the [Risk overview](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/peth#risk-overview) below. You have used the price-curve idea without knowing it: AMMs like Uniswap and Curve run on one, though their liquidity comes from LPs.

Two things make pETH's curve different from Pump.fun's:

1. **Purpose**: Pump.fun raises capital to seed liquidity; Polaris uses the curve to *back* pETH permanently.
2. **Temporality**: Pump.fun's curve terminates at "graduation"; the pETH curve is designed to keep running.

## What's unique about pETH's curve

pETH's curve is **two-way** (ETH ⇒ pETH and pETH ⇒ ETH) and gives pETH a price floor measured in ETH under the curve assumptions. The full floor and floor-ratio explanation lives on [pETH Floor Price](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/peth/floor-price).

Image: pETH bonding curve: ETH deposits create pETH along a power-law curve with a rising floor price

*Key takeaway: the curve is both the issuance path and the reserve-backed exit path; fees and conversion burns are designed to lift the floor.*

### The maths

The curve follows the formula:

$$
P = \alpha \cdot Q^{\beta}
$$

- **α**: a scaling parameter of little importance.
- **Q**: the supply of pETH.
- **β**: the power-law exponent that sets the curve's behaviour.

As supply `Q` increases, the price of pETH in ETH increases. **β** sets how tightly pETH tracks ETH: a large β decouples pETH and makes it more volatile; a small β couples them closely. Current values live on [Parameters](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/resources/testnet#parameters).

The Public Testnet 1 beta is testnet-only. The production beta remains pending; verify [Parameters](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/resources/testnet#parameters) before using any numeric value.

### How the production curve launches

Production launch is designed to initialize the curve through a dedicated **bootstrap** contract: a time-boxed, whitelisted deposit window with a minimum and maximum ETH raise. Depositors receive pETH when the raise initializes the curve and its starting floor - a portion claimable immediately, the rest vesting over a set period, with a penalized early-exit (rage-quit) option. Public Testnet 1 skipped this phase; its curve was initialized directly. Bootstrap terms and dates are pending on [Launch Status](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/launch-status).

## Interactive explorer

## pETH Beyond Positions

pETH is tailor-made collateral for Polaris pAsset markets and user positions, but it has a life of its own.

### Reserve loans and Split

You can [split pETH](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/peth/split) into **fpETH** (the floor leg) and **vpETH** (the premium leg). fpETH can collateralize **reserve loans** - WETH borrowed against floor-value, sized to the floor price so premium swings don't liquidate the position. See [pETH Split](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/peth/split) for the full mechanism, risks, and merge path.

### DeFi composability

pETH's rising floor and eventual deep ETH backing could make it a prime asset *outside* Polaris too. A pETH/ETH market on Aave, Morpho, or Euler is a natural fit, much like stETH across DeFi today.

## pETH vs stETH

stETH is a prime asset, but it lacks contract-native liquidity from its own reserve. Native withdrawals depend on the validator exit queue and can vary; DEX liquidity is deep, but depends on liquidity providers who can stop supplying in a panic.

Feature | **pETH** | **stETH**

Liquidity | Bonding-curve reserve, subject to contract and chain liveness | Deep, but LP-dependent
Withdrawal | Onchain sell path, with gas/slippage/MEV | Validator-exit queue; timing varies
Price floor | Contract-enforced under audited invariants | None
Main risk | Price risk vs ETH, bounded by the floor under the curve assumptions | LP/liquidity risk

stETH is a major ETH asset, but it does not provide pETH's reserve-backed onchain sell path.

## Risk overview

pETH carries three main risks:

1. **Technical risk**: the bonding curve is a smart contract; technical risk is unavoidable, mitigated through audits and modeling.
2. **Execution risk**: using the curve still requires Ethereum liveness, available gas, and transactions that can tolerate slippage and MEV.
3. **Price risk**: pETH is volatile against ETH. Your exposure depends on your entry price relative to the floor, i.e. the [floor ratio](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/peth/floor-price): a higher ratio means a smaller curve-implied drawdown before execution costs.

The curve design removes liquidity-provider withdrawal risk. That does not make execution riskless: the curve provides a contract-defined buy/sell path and floor while the relevant invariants hold.

Next steps:
- [The Floor Price](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/peth/floor-price): Understand the mathematically enforced minimum price of pETH.
- [Open a Position](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/minting/open-a-trove): Use pETH as collateral to issue your first pAsset.
- [Why Polaris](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/why-polaris): The design choices behind pETH, pAssets, and POLAR.

Relevant app/search vocabulary: Swap, swap, Split, fpETH, vpETH, pETH, floor price, pETH bonding curve, bonding curve, pETH floor.

---

## pETH Floor Price

URL: https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/peth/floor-price
Markdown URL: https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/peth/floor-price.md

## What it is

The **floor price** is pETH's mathematically enforced minimum price, in ETH, under the bonding-curve invariants. It is the curve price for the *last person out* if everyone exited the bonding curve at once - assuming the contracts work as designed, Ethereum is live, and users can pay execution costs. Because most exiters get a better curve price, it is a protocol worst case, not an average.

The floor is not a promise or a target defended by a treasury. It is a direct consequence of the bonding curve's reserves and supply, enforced by immutable code and dependent on the audited invariants holding.

## Why it is designed to rise

Two mechanisms push the floor up under the intended curve invariants. Normal protocol actions are not designed to push it down:

- **Swap fees feed the burn.** Every swap on the [bonding curve](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/peth) charges a pETH fee that accrues to the protocol's [Fee Router](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/stewardship/fee-router). Accumulated fee pETH is periodically **burned at the floor price**: supply falls while the reserve releases only the floor value of what was burned, so the floor rises. The released ETH is swapped back into pETH and funds the pETH yield flows.
- **Conversion auctions.** Issuing POLAR requires [burning pETH](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/polar/conversion-auctions), which again reduces supply and lifts the floor.

The result is a staircase under normal operation: pETH's market price fluctuates above the floor, while the floor itself steps upward over time.

Image: The rising pETH floor as a staircase stepping up over time, each step driven by a swap-fee burn or a conversion-auction burn that permanently reduces pETH supply

*Each swap-fee burn and conversion auction removes pETH supply, ratcheting the floor up a step - under the curve assumptions.*

## The floor ratio

The **floor ratio** expresses the floor as a percentage of the current market price:

$$
\text{Floor ratio} = \frac{\text{Floor price}}{\text{Market price}} \times 100
$$

It tells you the curve-implied drawdown to the floor before gas, slippage, MEV, and smart-contract or chain-liveness risk. A 90% floor ratio means 10% downside to the floor in ETH terms under those assumptions; 67% means 33% downside.

Floor ratio | Curve-implied drawdown to floor

90% | 10%
67% | 33%
55% | 45%

Image: The floor ratio: a volatile pETH market-price line above a steadily rising floor line; a worked example with market price 1.09 and floor 0.60 gives a 55% floor ratio and a 45% maximum drawdown to the floor

*A higher floor ratio means a smaller curve-implied drawdown to the floor - before gas, slippage, MEV, and technical risk.*

The floor ratio is itself volatile, driven by market sentiment and activity:

- In phases of **wild growth**, the market price runs far ahead of the floor, so the ratio can fall, sometimes below 50%.
- As the market price **normalizes**, the floor catches up and the ratio rises, until the limited remaining downside makes pETH attractive again.

## Assumptions behind the floor

Assumption | Why it matters

Bonding-curve contracts function as audited | The floor is a contract invariant, not an offchain guarantee
Ethereum remains live | Exiting requires a transaction that can be included
Users can pay gas | High gas can make small exits uneconomic
Slippage and MEV are tolerated or bounded | The quoted curve price and realized execution can differ
The relevant invariants stay unchanged and unbroken | The floor depends on reserves, supply accounting, and burn/create rules

> Your risk in pETH is decided at entry: the spread between your entry price and the floor (the floor ratio), plus execution and technical risk. Under the intended curve invariant, normal protocol activity moves the floor up, not down.

Next steps:
- [Split: fpETH & vpETH](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/peth/split): Separate the floor-backed claim from the premium upside.
- [Conversion Auctions](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/polar/conversion-auctions): The pETH burn that ratchets the floor and creates POLAR.
- [Risk Disclosure](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/resources/risk-disclosure): What the floor does and does not protect you from.

Relevant app/search vocabulary: Swap, swap, Split, fpETH, vpETH, pETH, floor price, pETH bonding curve, bonding curve, pETH floor.

---

## pETH Split: fpETH and vpETH

URL: https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/peth/split
Markdown URL: https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/peth/split.md

The **Split** mechanism separates pETH into two matching tokens:

```
1 pETH -> 1 fpETH + 1 vpETH
```

Splitting does not create extra value. It separates the pETH position into a **floor leg** and a **premium leg** so users can hold, trade, merge, or borrow against the part of pETH they actually want.

## The two legs

Token | Meaning | Main use

**fpETH** | Floor-price pETH. It tracks the pETH floor-value claim under the splitter and bonding-curve assumptions. | Hold floor carry, redeem for WETH through the splitter, or use as collateral in reserve loans.
**vpETH** | Volatility-premium pETH. It represents exposure to pETH's market value above the floor. | Hold or trade the premium/upside leg after separating out the floor claim.

Think of pETH as:

```
pETH value = floor value + premium above floor
```

fpETH isolates the floor-value side. vpETH isolates the remaining premium side. If pETH trades close to its floor, vpETH can become worth very little. If pETH trades far above its floor, vpETH carries most of that premium exposure.

For intuition, with pETH at a market price of 1.00 ETH:

Floor price | [Floor ratio](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/peth/floor-price) | fpETH claim (floor leg) | vpETH exposure (premium leg)

0.60 ETH | 60% | ≈ 0.60 ETH | ≈ 0.40 ETH
0.90 ETH | 90% | ≈ 0.90 ETH | ≈ 0.10 ETH

The arithmetic is indicative: each leg trades on its own market, so realized prices can deviate from the decomposition.

## Split and merge

**Split** deposits pETH into the splitter contract and sends equal amounts of fpETH and vpETH to your wallet.

**Merge** burns equal amounts of fpETH and vpETH and releases the matching pETH from the splitter.

You need both legs in equal amounts to merge back into pETH. If you sold or deposited one leg, you must reacquire it before merging.

## fpETH redemption

fpETH can be redeemed through the splitter for WETH at the current pETH floor-price value under the contract assumptions. That redemption is the reason fpETH is the **floor leg**.

This is not an offchain guarantee. It depends on the splitter, bonding curve, reserve accounting, Ethereum liveness, gas availability, and the relevant invariants holding.

## Reserve loans

Reserve loans use **fpETH** as collateral, and each loan is recorded onchain as an NFT. The borrowable WETH amount is based on the fpETH amount and current pETH floor price, not on pETH's market premium.

That design reduces price-liquidation risk from pETH premium swings, but it does not make the position risk-free:

- Debt can accrue according to the reserve-loan fee model.
- Closing or adjusting can be affected by floor-price changes before inclusion.
- You still need WETH to repay and unlock fpETH.
- Smart-contract, approval, oracle, gas, and chain-liveness risks remain.

Reserve loans are separate from pAsset positions. A position issues pUSD, pGOLD, or another pAsset against pETH collateral. A reserve loan borrows WETH against fpETH.

## Floor carry

**Floor carry** is the return a fpETH holder may realize as the pETH floor rises. If the floor price increases while you hold fpETH, the floor-value redemption claim increases under the contract assumptions.

Floor carry is not a fixed APR. It depends on actual floor growth from protocol activity, execution costs, market pricing of fpETH, and whether the redemption path is available when you use it.

## Related app tabs

- [Split](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/using-app/split) for splitting and merging.
- [Borrow](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/using-app/borrow) for reserve loans.
- [Dashboard](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/using-app) for live token prices; the floor and market price show on the Split tab.

Relevant app/search vocabulary: Swap, swap, Split, fpETH, vpETH, pETH, floor price, pETH bonding curve, bonding curve, pETH floor.

---

## POLAR Token, Staking, and Conversion

URL: https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/polar
Markdown URL: https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/polar.md

POLAR is Polaris's stewardship token and outward-facing growth lever. After the initial distribution, new POLAR is intended to enter through one path: [burn pETH in a conversion auction](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/polar/conversion-auctions).

App anchors: **Earn** for current POLAR Staking where exposed, and **Advanced** or a conversion panel for pETH -> POLAR conversion actions.

> **Status distinction:** The app may expose **POLAR Staking** as a current stake/unstake/claim surface on testnet. **vePOLAR locking** is separate and forthcoming - see [POLAR Staking vs vePOLAR](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/polar/tokenomics#polar-staking-vs-vepolar) for what is committed and what is pending. Do not use a conversion, staking, or locking interface unless [Launch Status](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/launch-status) and [Contracts & Addresses](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/resources/testnet#contracts-and-addresses) point to it.

## Why a third token

Polaris runs on three engines: pETH, the pAssets, and POLAR. The first two handle everything the protocol *needs* to function: pETH backs pAsset markets and user positions, Earn Vaults generate yield, interest rates self-adjust, and liquidations process autonomously. **Polaris works without POLAR.**

So why add a third token? Because functioning and scaling differ. The design splits along an **inward- vs. outward-facing** logic:

Function | Handled by | Examples

**Inward-facing** - sustain the core | pETH + pAssets | interest rates, floor growth, Earn Vault incentivization, liquidations
**Outward-facing** - grow the ecosystem | POLAR | external DEX liquidity, Flow onboarding, [StablecoinOS](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/minting/passet-catalog#stablecoinos-an-open-catalog) coordination

POLAR is deliberately separated from the core so the core's sustainability never depends on it. If POLAR incentives dried up tomorrow, pUSD could still be issued, the bonding curve would still function, and Earn Vaults would still pay yield. The base protocol does not need POLAR to survive, but it scales far better with it.

## Created only by burning pETH

There is no planned team creation function or calendar inflation schedule disconnected from protocol activity. The detailed economics live on [POLAR Tokenomics](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/polar/tokenomics); the auction mechanics live on [Conversion Auctions](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/polar/conversion-auctions).

## POLAR Staking and future vePOLAR

POLAR can be put to work two ways: current **POLAR Staking** where exposed, and forthcoming **[vePOLAR](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/stewardship/vepolar)** locking for stewardship rights. See [POLAR Tokenomics](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/polar/tokenomics#polar-staking-vs-vepolar) for the full distinction.

## Go deeper

Page | What it answers

[POLAR Tokenomics](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/polar/tokenomics) | Issuance structure, current staking, future vePOLAR, and what is still pending.
[POLAR Conversion Auctions](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/polar/conversion-auctions) | How spike-and-decay pricing, pETH burns, rebates, and floor growth work, plus how to convert.
[vePOLAR Locking and Stewardship](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/stewardship/vepolar) | The forthcoming vote-escrow layer and its stewardship role.

POLAR is not a claim on a treasury or a vote in an unbounded DAO. It is the token used for outward-facing growth and bounded stewardship.

Next steps:
- [POLAR Staking](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/using-app/earn): Current stake, unstake, and claim surface where exposed.
- [Conversion Auctions](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/polar/conversion-auctions): How spike-and-decay pricing, rebates, and floor growth work.
- [Stewardship](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/stewardship): Bounded parameter control and future vePOLAR.

Relevant app/search vocabulary: POLAR, convert, lock, vePOLAR, burn pETH, conversion auction, POLAR token, POLAR staking, POLAR tokenomics.

---

## POLAR Conversion Auctions

URL: https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/polar/conversion-auctions
Markdown URL: https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/polar/conversion-auctions.md

Conversion auctions let converters burn pETH to receive new POLAR. This page covers the auction mechanics; POLAR's broader economics live on [POLAR Tokenomics](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/polar/tokenomics).

Image: The conversion mechanism: a converter burns pETH, receives new POLAR, lifts the pETH floor price, and releases ETH from the bonding-curve reserve; the released ETH splits into a converter rebate and pETH forwarded to the Fee Router

## Spike-and-decay pricing

Conversion runs as a **continuous, permissionless auction**. Anyone can convert pETH to POLAR at any moment; whether it is *profitable* depends on the conversion price versus POLAR's market price. The price follows a **spike-and-decay** model:

1. The conversion price **continuously decays**. The longer nobody converts, the cheaper the offered price - and the more attractive conversion gets.
2. When a conversion happens, the price **spikes upward**, proportional to the fraction of POLAR supply just inflated. Issuing 1% of supply spikes the price far more than issuing 0.01%.
3. After the spike, decay resumes until the next conversion.

The result is a natural rhythm: the price decays until an arbitrageur sees it drift below POLAR's market price, converts to capture the spread, and spikes it back up - closing the opportunity until decay reopens it.

Image: Spike-and-decay conversion pricing: the conversion price decays continuously until it drops below POLAR's market price, opening an arbitrage window; each conversion spikes the price up by an amount proportional to the fraction of POLAR supply created, then decay resumes

> **Note:**
> The spike is computed against **POLAR supply, not pETH supply**. This steers conversions toward small, frequent operations: a whale converting a large amount in one go faces rapidly diminishing returns as each increment spikes the price against them. The mechanism self-regulates toward steady, incremental issuing.

> *Exact decay and spike parameters are finalized closer to launch, set through stewardship. The converter rebate is bounded between 0% and 100%; final decay and spike bounds are pending.*

## What burning pETH does

Burning pETH through a conversion has two effects beyond issuing POLAR:

- **ETH is released from the bonding curve reserve.** The ETH backing the burned pETH is freed as conversion gain.
- **The [floor price](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/peth/floor-price) can rise under the curve assumptions.** Reducing supply while preserving reserve backing lifts the modeled floor. Conversions are one-way: POLAR cannot be converted back to pETH.

Image: The floor-price ratchet: burning pETH through conversions contracts supply along the power-law curve, lifting the floor from one level to a higher one; because conversions are one-way, the floor steps up and does not come back down

See [pETH Floor Price](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/peth/floor-price) for the full floor and floor-ratio explanation.

## The converter rebate

Before released ETH becomes system yield, a slice can return to the converter. The **converter rebate** is a stewardship-controlled dial between conversion volume and system yield:

Rebate | Effect

**0%** | Maximum yield to the system; conversions cost the most. Sensible when POLAR's market cap exceeds pETH's.
**100%** | Zero system yield; conversions are cheapest - the rebate returns the burned pETH's floor value, so the converter's net cost is only the premium above floor (at a 60% [floor ratio](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/peth/floor-price), roughly 40% of the pETH's market value). An emergency lever to drive volume when the floor is stalling.
**In between** | The usual operating range, shifting with market conditions.

Stewards watch the **POLAR / pETH market-cap ratio**: when POLAR's is larger, a lower rebate is easier to defend; when it flips, higher rebates may support conversion volume.

> **Note:**
> The total ETH released from the curve is **unaffected** by the rebate in the model. The floor-price impact is the same however the ETH is split afterward, assuming the contracts and curve math execute as designed. The rebate reshapes the *distribution* of value, not the underlying collateral dynamics.

After the rebate, the remaining ETH is swapped into pETH and forwarded to the **[Fee Router](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/stewardship/fee-router)**, which buffers it and distributes the yield to pETH positions, proportionally to their recorded debt.

## What conversion does *not* do

- **It does not manufacture demand for POLAR.** If POLAR trades below the conversion price, no conversions happen until decay catches up.
- **Parameters carry weight.** Decay too fast can cause frequent dilution; decay too slow can stall conversion activity. Final guardrails remain pending.

## How to convert

Conversions are a continuous, permissionless auction tuned for arbitrage bots and MEV searchers, so converting profitably means competing with them. Most users will hold POLAR, use current POLAR Staking where the app exposes it, or wait for [vePOLAR](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/stewardship/vepolar) locking instead of converting directly.

**You need** pETH to burn (bought on the [bonding curve](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/peth) or a DEX) and a wallet connected to the Polaris app. On Public Testnet 1 this is Sepolia-only; production conversions are not actionable until [Launch Status](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/launch-status) publishes the official app, conversion contract, final parameters, and audit status.

### Open the conversion panel

It shows the live **conversion price** and POLAR's **market price** side by side.

### Watch the decaying price

The conversion price decays continuously and spikes upward each time someone converts. A conversion is profitable when it drifts **below** POLAR's market price; that spread is your margin.

### Read the quote

Enter a pETH amount. The panel previews the **POLAR you will receive**, the **ETH rebate** at the current [rebate](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/polar/conversion-auctions#the-converter-rebate) setting, and the resulting **price spike**. Because the spike scales with the fraction of POLAR supply created, larger amounts price progressively worse; favor smaller increments.

### Decide

Convert only while the previewed POLAR (plus rebate) beats acquiring POLAR on the open market. If the spread has closed, wait for decay to reopen it.

### Burn pETH ⇒ receive POLAR

Confirm the transaction. Your pETH is **permanently burned**, you receive POLAR, and any ETH rebate goes to your wallet.

### Stake, future-lock, or hold

Stake POLAR where the current app exposes POLAR Staking, wait for [vePOLAR](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/stewardship/vepolar) locking once audited deployment publishes it, or hold it.

Relevant app/search vocabulary: POLAR, convert, lock, vePOLAR, burn pETH, conversion auction.

---

## POLAR Tokenomics

URL: https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/polar/tokenomics
Markdown URL: https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/polar/tokenomics.md

This page consolidates POLAR's economics. Each piece has its own page; here they sit side by side. For the reasoning behind the token, read [The POLAR Token](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/polar); for the issuance mechanism, [Conversion Auctions](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/polar/conversion-auctions).

> **Current status:** POLAR's total supply, distribution schedule, and vesting terms are pending and will be published at launch. This page describes the *structure* of POLAR's economics, fixed by design, not the final numbers, which are forthcoming. Treat any figure not stated here as not yet finalized.

## Issuance: created only by burning pETH

After the initial distribution, new POLAR is intended to enter circulation only by burning pETH in a [conversion auction](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/polar/conversion-auctions). There is no planned team creation function or calendar-based inflation schedule.

- Every POLAR that enters circulation after launch is intended to require someone to burn pETH.
- Conversion runs as a continuous, permissionless [spike-and-decay auction](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/polar/conversion-auctions), so issuance tracks real arbitrage activity, not a fixed emission curve. Most conversions are executed by arbitrage bots and MEV searchers.

Each conversion burns pETH, releases ETH from the bonding-curve reserve as yield, and creates POLAR. See [pETH Floor Price](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/peth/floor-price) for the floor mechanics.

## A bounded, productive dilution

Every conversion dilutes existing POLAR holders, but the dilution is bounded and buys a concrete improvement:

- **Bounded.** [Spike-and-decay pricing](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/polar/conversion-auctions) makes large or rapid issuing throttle itself, so issuance tracks arbitrage demand. Final parameter bounds are pending.
- **Productive.** Each unit of dilution corresponds to burned pETH and released ETH yield, unlike emissions disconnected from protocol activity.
- **Progressive for the team.** The founding team's outsized early share is diluted by the same mechanism: the faster Polaris grows, the more attractive conversions become, and the more that initial share shrinks. The dilution is enforced by code, not pledged in a forum.

## POLAR Staking vs vePOLAR

The app vocabulary matters:

- **POLAR Staking** is the current staking surface where a deployment exposes stake, unstake, and claim actions for routed rewards.
- **[vePOLAR](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/stewardship/vepolar)** is the forthcoming vote-escrow layer. Locking POLAR for a period is intended to return vePOLAR, which grants stewardship rights and a yield path.

vePOLAR proposal rules and the final yield split are pending; the committed lock design (durations, decay, delegation) lives on [vePOLAR](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/stewardship/vepolar#lock-status). Do not treat current POLAR Staking as a substitute for vePOLAR voting rights.

## Where the yield comes from

POLAR's value accrual runs on hard-asset protocol revenue, buffered and routed by the **[Fee Router](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/stewardship/fee-router)** (pETH) and, separately, by the protocol's interest router (pAsset). It reaches these destinations:

Destination | Routed by | What they receive

**Positions** | Fee Router (pETH) | pETH yield, credited proportionally to recorded debt
**POLAR stakers** | Interest router (pAsset) | A stewarded share of protocol interest where the staking surface is live
**vePOLAR lockers** | Pending deployment | Forthcoming stewardship and yield path after audited locking deployment
**Flow recipients** | Fee Router, via [Flows](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/stewardship/fee-router) | Revenue-sharing for whitelisted contracts (forthcoming)

Inputs are protocol revenue, not emitted POLAR. Exact destination splits, production staking terms, and vePOLAR locking terms are pending.

## What POLAR does *not* have

The structure deliberately omits the usual extraction surfaces:

- **No gauges.** Stewardship cannot create POLAR emissions gauges.
- **No bribes.** No vote-buying markets are routed through the protocol.
- **No emissions.** Ecosystem incentives flow through [Flows](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/stewardship/fee-router) as hard-asset revenue sharing, funded by protocol revenue, not token emissions.
- **No planned creation outside conversion.** Final deployment is intended to create POLAR only through pETH conversion.

## The Polar Council

The **Polar Council** is a planned treasury-funded program for onboarding and ecosystem contributors, comparable in spirit to veAERO's Flight School. Initially team-managed; its allocation, operating framework, eligibility, and reporting requirements are pending publication.

Next steps:
- [Conversion Auctions](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/polar/conversion-auctions): The spike-and-decay mechanism behind POLAR conversion.
- [vePOLAR Locking](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/stewardship/vepolar): The committed lock design and forthcoming stewardship rights.
- [Fee Router & Flows](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/stewardship/fee-router): How the revenue in the table above is buffered and routed.

---

**[Go deeper →](https://polarisfinance.io/blog/)**

Relevant app/search vocabulary: POLAR, convert, lock, vePOLAR, burn pETH, conversion auction, POLAR token, POLAR staking, POLAR tokenomics.

---

## pAsset Redemptions

URL: https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/redemptions-liquidations
Markdown URL: https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/redemptions-liquidations.md

> **Actionability:** Redemptions are testnet-actionable only on Sepolia using test assets and current manifests. Production redemptions are not mainnet-actionable until [Polaris Launch Status](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/launch-status) publishes the official app, production addresses, final fee formula, and audit status.

## What a redemption is

A **redemption** exchanges a pAsset for the **[pETH](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/peth)** collateral backing it at the pAsset's protocol reference value, minus a fee. It pays when the pAsset trades *below* peg by more than the fee, gas, slippage, and execution risk.

If pUSD slips to \$0.98, a redeemer can buy pUSD cheaply, redeem it for about \$1 of pETH minus the fee, and sell or hold the pETH. The pETH comes from the collateral of positions carrying that pUSD debt, and their debt shrinks by the pUSD redeemed. No counterparty agrees to the trade; the protocol enforces it.

This puts an arbitrage floor under the pAsset's market price.

## Parameters

Redemption fees, decay factors, and current network values live on [Parameters](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/resources/testnet#parameters). This page explains the mechanism; the parameter page owns the numbers.

## How it restores the peg

A below-peg pAsset is a standing arbitrage. Walk the loop:

1. **Buy** pUSD on the market at \$0.98.
2. **Redeem** it for \$1 − fee of pETH.
3. **Sell** the pETH, pocket the spread.
4. Repeat until the arb closes.

Each pass buys pUSD on the open market, applying **upward price pressure**. Arbitrageurs keep going until pUSD is back at peg and the trade stops paying. Self-interest restores the peg, not intervention.

> **Note:** Redemptions handle the *below*-peg case. The *above*-peg case is handled by the inverse mechanism, **issuing**, where arbitrageurs add pETH to positions to issue pAssets when the pAsset trades over peg. See [Issuing pAssets](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/minting).

The two mechanisms are mirror images:

Mechanism | Direction | Arbitrageur does | Effect on positions

**Redemption** | pAsset < peg | Burns pAsset (repays debt) ⇒ takes pETH | Debt and collateral both shrink
**Issuing** | pAsset > peg | Adds pETH ⇒ issues pAsset | Debt and collateral both grow

Their volumes also serve as the protocol's peg signal: a spike in redemptions reads as a downward depeg, and a spike in issuance reads as an upward one. That is how Polaris sets [autonomous interest rates](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/minting) without a direct pUSD market-price feed.

Image: Interest rate and peg stability: above peg, issuing volume rises and the interest rate falls to expand supply; below peg, redemption volume rises and the interest rate climbs to contract supply - each loop pushing the price back toward peg

*Redemption and issuance volume are the onchain peg signal; the loops above show the mechanism's direction, not exact arithmetic.*

## Mechanics in detail

### Fees and fill limits

Current fee floors, decay factors, and fill constraints live on [Parameters](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/resources/testnet#parameters). The user-facing rule is simple: the redeemer receives the redeemed amount's reference value in pETH **minus** the fee and execution costs. If the fee, gas, pETH slippage, MEV risk, or price movement exceed the below-peg spread, the redemption is unprofitable.

Two contract-level details refine that rule. The fee is not paid out to anyone: the collateral it represents stays in the branch, accruing proportionally to the remaining positions. And if the branch's [reserve-to-debt ratio (TCR)](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/redemptions-liquidations/recovery-mode) is below 100%, the post-fee proceeds are scaled down by that ratio - redeeming against a thinly backed branch returns proportionally less pETH.

The app should show a pre-confirmation quote once the interface is live. Treat examples here as arithmetic only.

### Borrower impact

A redemption targets the whole pAsset system, not a specific position. It reduces debt and pETH collateral across **all** open positions for that pAsset, proportionally to each position's recorded collateral. There is no LTV queue, no "riskiest position first," and no health metric that exempts a position.

Being redeemed against is not a liquidation penalty: your debt is repaid at face value with your own collateral, so net equity is broadly unchanged before fees and rounding. The cost is control. Your position becomes smaller, your pETH exposure falls, and future pETH-linked yield potential falls with it.

One edge case: because both reductions are shared based on collateral, a position holding a lot of collateral relative to its debt can shed more debt than it owes. Debt floors at zero, and the protocol sends the overshoot to the position owner as pAsset the next time the position is touched.

Lower LTV protects against **liquidation**, not redemption. It can still matter after a redemption, because the remaining LTV often improves but the position is smaller.

### MEV and transaction ordering

Redemptions are visible onchain transactions. Searchers can compete for the same spread, the pETH exit price can move, and system totals can shift before your transaction lands. Use the app's slippage limits, fee quotes, and any private-transaction support.

## Worked example: redemption impact

Assume pUSD trades at \$0.98, the redemption fee quote is 0.5%, and gas/slippage are ignored. A redeemer buys 10,000 pUSD for \$9,800 and redeems it for \$10,000 of pETH minus the 0.5% fee, or about \$9,950 of pETH. The gross spread is about \$150 before gas, slippage, MEV, and price movement.

Now the borrower side of the same redemption. The 10,000 pUSD redeemed reduces total system debt by 10,000 pUSD and removes roughly \$10,000 of pETH from the pool. Both reductions are shared across every open position in proportion to recorded collateral.

For intuition, here is the proportional share for one position holding a fifth of the system's collateral, so it absorbs a fifth of the redemption (2,000 pUSD of debt and about \$2,000 of pETH):

Step | Collateral value | Debt | LTV

Before redemption | \$18,000 | 12,000 pUSD | 66.7%
After absorbing its share | About \$16,000 | 10,000 pUSD | 62.5%

The borrower did not repay voluntarily; the market did it through the redemption path. The position is slightly safer by LTV, but smaller and less exposed to pETH.

## Before you act

1. Track your **LTV** with enough buffer for price moves, interest accrual, and redemption impact.
2. Treat a below-peg pAsset as a live signal that redemptions may shrink your position.

---

## Next actions

- [Managing Your Position](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/minting/managing-your-trove) for borrower levers.
- [Liquidations](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/redemptions-liquidations/liquidations) for the forced-close path.
- [Recovery Mode](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/redemptions-liquidations/recovery-mode) for the system-wide defensive state.
- [Risk Disclosure](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/resources/risk-disclosure) for the full risk model.
- [Parameters](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/resources/testnet#parameters) for current redemption values.

---

## Polaris Liquidations

URL: https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/redemptions-liquidations/liquidations
Markdown URL: https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/redemptions-liquidations/liquidations.md

## What a liquidation is

A **liquidation** closes a position whose **LTV exceeds the maximum** - the point at which its collateral no longer comfortably covers its debt. The protocol cancels the position's debt and seizes its pETH collateral.

The purpose is solvency. Every pAsset is a claim on collateral; leaving undercollateralized positions open would leave the pAsset not fully backed. Liquidations are how Polaris is designed to keep backing intact, subject to functioning contracts, live Ethereum execution, available gas, valid pricing data, keeper/executor availability, and audited invariants.

Unlike a [redemption](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/redemptions-liquidations), a liquidation is not an arbitrage you can opt into at par - it is a forced close triggered by your own LTV crossing a hard line.

## Where the debt and collateral go

Liquidation runs in two tiers.

1. **The Earn Vault absorbs it first.** The [Earn Vault](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/yield) holds a reserve of the pAsset. When a position is liquidated, the vault burns pAsset equal to the position's debt and receives pETH collateral covering the debt plus the liquidation penalty (any surplus goes back to the borrower - see below). Because liquidated positions are typically seized while still *overcollateralized* but past the LTV liquidation line, the vault usually receives more value in pETH than the pAsset it burns. That surplus is the depositors' compensation.

2. **Redistribution is the fallback.** If the Earn Vault is empty or too small to cover the debt, the position's debt *and* collateral spread across the remaining positions on that pAsset, in proportion to their size. Other borrowers take on a slice of debt and a matching slice of pETH. The system stays solvent either way.

Path | Trigger | Absorbs debt | Receives collateral

**Earn Vault** | Vault has sufficient pAsset | Vault burns its pAsset | Depositors, based on their share
**Redistribution** | Vault empty or insufficient | Other positions take it on | Other positions, based on size

**Collateral surplus.** When the Earn Vault absorbs a liquidation, the position surrenders only what covers its debt, a liquidation penalty, and gas compensation for the liquidator. Any collateral beyond that goes to the **Collateral Surplus Pool**, where the former borrower can claim it. The claim is an explicit transaction - the surplus is not pushed back automatically, and it waits in the pool until claimed. (Collateral spread through the redistribution fallback is not returned this way.)

## Values to check

Current MCR, Recovery Mode, fee, and liquidation values live on [Parameters](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/resources/testnet#parameters). This page explains the closeout path; the parameter page owns the numbers.

Before acting, check:

- The pAsset's max LTV or inverse minimum collateral ratio.
- Any liquidation penalty, gas compensation, and keeper incentive.
- Earn Vault coverage and whether redistribution can apply.
- Oracle freshness and fallback behavior.

## Worked example: liquidation

The MCR number below is the current Public Testnet 1 pUSD/pGOLD value as of June 8, 2026. Parameters publish this as a 115% minimum collateral ratio, equivalent to about 87.0% max LTV. A borrower has 10,000 pUSD of debt and pETH collateral valued at \$11,300, so the LTV is about 88.5% - above the current testnet maximum, and liquidatable.

If the Earn Vault is large enough, it burns 10,000 pUSD and receives the position's pETH. The borrower's debt is cleared; collateral covers the debt plus the contract-defined liquidation penalty and gas compensation where available, with any surplus returned for the borrower to claim. Depositors receive pETH proportionally; in a normal overcollateralized liquidation, that pETH should be worth more than the pUSD burned, but the exact gain depends on the penalty, execution timing, pETH price, gas, and slippage.

If the Earn Vault is empty or too small, the uncovered portion follows the redistribution path: remaining positions inherit a proportional slice of both the liquidated debt and collateral.

## Risks

Liquidation is involuntary and can happen fast. A liquidated position gives up its collateral to cover the debt plus a liquidation penalty and gas compensation; any surplus is returned to you to claim, but the penalty makes liquidation a real loss to avoid. Keep a **buffer** below max LTV so a sharp move in collateral price does not cross the line. The thinner your margin, the less room you have when volatility hits. See Managing Your Position.

You are the system's first-loss backstop. In a flash crash, positions can be liquidated near - or in extreme cases below - 100% collateralization, and you may receive pETH worth less than the pAsset burned. Over a full cycle the surplus from healthy liquidations compensates for that risk, but it is not riskless yield. See The Earn Vault and the Risk Disclosure.

## How liquidation interacts with system health

A position's LTV is not all that matters. When system-wide backing runs thin, the protocol enters [Recovery Mode](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/redemptions-liquidations/recovery-mode), which tightens risk-increasing borrower operations while it lasts. Liquidation eligibility is unchanged: a position is liquidated whenever its LTV exceeds the maximum, in every mode. Recovery Mode is the system-level safeguard complementing the position-level liquidation described here.

Next steps:
- [Managing Your Position](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/minting/managing-your-trove): Keep your LTV healthy and avoid liquidation.
- [Earn Vaults](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/yield): Understand how vaults absorb liquidations.
- [Recovery Mode](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/redemptions-liquidations/recovery-mode): The system-level safeguard for thin backing.

Relevant app/search vocabulary: liquidation, liquidations, liquidate, liquidated, liquidation penalty.

---

## Polaris Recovery Mode

URL: https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/redemptions-liquidations/recovery-mode
Markdown URL: https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/redemptions-liquidations/recovery-mode.md

## The Total Collateralization Ratio

The **Total Collateralization Ratio (TCR)** is the system-wide health metric for a pAsset. It is not a simple market-value-of-collateral over debt figure: it measures the bonding-curve reserve backing all the pETH collateral - the collateral scaled by the curve's reserve-backing fraction and valued at the reserve (redemption) price - divided by total pAsset debt outstanding.

$$
\text{TCR} = \frac{\text{Reserve backing the collateral} \times \text{reserve price}}{\text{Total debt}}
$$

A high TCR means a deep, well-backed system; a low TCR means the reserve behind that pAsset is thin relative to its debt. In the contracts this is the reserve-to-debt ratio, and crossing the threshold triggers the protective state (internally called Emergency Mode).

## What Recovery Mode is

**Recovery Mode** is a protective state entered automatically when TCR drops below a set threshold. It is not a freeze or shutdown; it tightens borrower actions that would make system backing thinner.

## Parameters

Recovery Mode thresholds and current per-pAsset values live on [Parameters](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/resources/testnet#parameters). This page explains behavior, not current numbers.

## How behavior changes

Recovery Mode does **not** change which positions are liquidatable. In every mode, a position is liquidatable only once its LTV exceeds the normal maximum. What tightens is the set of borrower actions allowed while the state is active.

Your action | Normal Mode | Recovery Mode

**Add collateral / repay debt** | Allowed | Allowed
**Withdraw collateral / issue more debt** | Allowed if LTV stays below the maximum | Allowed only if the resulting LTV stays below the stricter recovery maximum and does not increase your LTV
**Liquidation eligibility** | LTV above the maximum | LTV above the maximum (unchanged)

The principle: actions that strengthen backing stay open; actions that thin backing face a higher bar. In practice, a collateral withdrawal or new issuance on its own always raises LTV, so in Recovery Mode it goes through only as part of a combined adjustment that offsets it - adding collateral or repaying debt in the same transaction. Current trigger and action values are published on [Parameters](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/resources/testnet#parameters).
